text:
Short- and long-chain per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) are a group of persistent groundwater contaminants of emerging concern. In this study, short- and long-chain PFAS removal efficiency using GAC filtration and IEX treatment was investigated in waterworks scenario. Laboratory batch experiments and pilot-scale column tests were carried out using a range of adsorbents in a contaminated groundwater matrix. Overall, regardless of the type of adsorbent used, it was observed that the short-chain perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs) had lower sorption affinity than their long-chain homologues and short-chain perfluoroalkyl sulfonic acids (PFSAs).

In the pilot trials, GAC was ineffective for the mobile short-chain compounds PFBA and PFPeA. The breakthrough levels were 10% at relatively low throughputs of 6000 and 11000 bed volumes (BV), respectively. Although there were instances where the ion exchange media was significantly more effective – for example, the 10% breakthrough was at 5000 BV for PFBA, 10000 BV for PFPeA and 18000 BV for PFHxA – their higher unit cost makes them an unattractive, if not economically speaking, a "single-use" alternative to GAC.

An economically viable process using the IEX system for wastewater treatment is obtained after integrating the resin regeneration stage. Moreover, while the need to add ethanol to the regeneration brine to remove the long-chain PFAS complicates the process for removal of these contaminants, the short-chain PFAS such as PFBA and PFPeA can be removed from the weakly basic ion exchangers using aqueous regeneration solutions without the need for catalysts or additional chemicals, which makes the regenerated IEX system for removal of these contaminants more economical and sustainable than activated carbon systems for the treatment of the complex PFAS plumes that are difficult to manage.
explanation: The text uses coherent, domain-specific PFAS terminology and plausible experimental details, which could come from a real study but also resemble AI-generated abstracts that imitate scientific prose. The absence of clear references or niche errors keeps the verdict uncertain, with a slight tilt toward AI authorship.
